purpose: Named presets a solution spec can adopt instead of hand-writing everything: scaffold presets (file layout, tests, commands) and security baselines (the rules a solution must obey).
howToUse: In your solution spec's scaffold block, set boilerplate.answer to a scaffold preset name; in its security block, set baseline.answer to a security baseline name. Scaffold fields you fill in yourself override the preset. Security rules you add are ADDITIONS — they can never weaken the baseline. Versions are pinned in each preset so scaffolding is deliberate — update them here to move; if a preset doesn't pin something, the AI uses the latest stable release and records the exact version in its report-back. These are starting points — edit this file or add your own to match how your team really works.

rules: HTTPS only — the site is served over HTTPS and never links to or loads plain-HTTP content.
No secrets in code or in the browser bundle — anything shipped to the browser is public; the front end may only hold non-secret settings (like the API's URL) referenced by name.
Never log, display, or store credentials or tokens in plain text (including console logs and error reports).
All user input is treated as untrusted — rely on the framework's escaping; never build HTML from raw input.
Auth tokens are sent only to this project's own API, over HTTPS, and never appear in URLs.
Dependencies are installed from the lockfile; new dependencies are named in the plan for approval.
HTTPS only — plain-HTTP requests are rejected or redirected, never served.
No secrets in code, config, or logs — settings are referenced by name from the infrastructure settings list.
Every endpoint requires authentication unless the endpoint brief explicitly says it is public.
Every operation enforces the roles/claims its endpoint brief declares — deny by default.
All input is validated before use; requests that fail validation get a 4xx, not a crash.
Error responses are generic — no stack traces, no internal details, and no hint whether a username or password was the wrong half of a login.
CORS is restricted to the project's own front-end origins — never a wildcard with credentials.
Dependencies are installed from the lockfile; new dependencies are named in the plan for approval.
Connections are encrypted in transit (TLS); unencrypted connections are refused.
Data is encrypted at rest where the engine supports it.
No credentials in code — applications connect using named settings from their infrastructure settings list.
Least privilege — application accounts can read/write their own data only; they are never database admins.
All access from application code uses parameterised queries — never SQL built from strings.
Fields marked sensitive in a database-model brief are stored hashed or encrypted, exactly as the brief says.

embedded-database-baseline.description: For a database engine embedded in the application process (e.g. SQLite, Turso/libSQL, DuckDB) rather than reached over a network.
rules: The database file lives inside the application's own data directory — never a world-readable or shared location.
Data marked sensitive in a database-model brief is encrypted at rest, using the embedded engine's own encryption if it has one.
No credentials hard-coded — if the engine supports an at-rest encryption key, it is a named setting, never a literal in code.
All access from application code uses parameterised queries — never SQL built from strings.
The application opens the database with the least privilege the engine allows for normal operation (no elevated/admin mode).

application-baseline.description: For a standalone executable (CLI, TUI, or desktop GUI) rather than a hosted website or API.
rules: No secrets in code or config — settings are referenced by name from the infrastructure settings list, never hard-coded.
All file paths built from user input are validated — no path traversal outside the application's own data directories.
Any network calls the application makes use HTTPS only.
Untrusted input (opened files, pasted content, dragged-in items, CLI arguments) is never executed or interpreted as code.
Unsafe code (Rust `unsafe` blocks) is avoided; where required, it is isolated, documented with the invariant it relies on, and called out in the plan for approval.
Dependencies are installed from the lockfile (Cargo.lock); new dependencies are named in the plan for approval.
Crash reports and logs never include user data or secrets.

react-vite.description: TypeScript + React on Vite, tested with Vitest + React Testing Library. Works with Tailwind or plain CSS.
fileLayout: src/pages/ — one file per page, named after the page brief (CamelCase)
src/components/ — shared UI components
src/lib/ — shared helpers (apiClient, auth)
src/pages/__tests__/ and src/lib/__tests__/ — tests beside what they test
public/ — static assets
index.html, vite.config.ts, tsconfig.json — project config

testing.kinds: Unit tests for lib helpers and components; per page, one test for each check in the page brief's tests answer.

go-azure-functions.description: Go HTTP handlers hosted on Azure Functions (custom handler), table-driven tests with the standard library.
fileLayout: cmd/api/main.go — entry point and router
internal/handlers/ — one file per resource, matching the endpoint briefs
internal/auth/ — JWT validation and role checks
internal/models/ — request/response structs
internal/handlers/*_test.go — table-driven tests beside each handler
host.json, local.settings.json — Azure Functions config

testing.kinds: Table-driven tests per handler: happy path, auth required, wrong role — one case per operation in the endpoint brief.

dotnet-webapi.description: C# ASP.NET Core Web API tested with xUnit and WebApplicationFactory.
fileLayout: src/<Solution>.Api/Controllers/ — one controller per resource, matching the endpoint briefs
src/<Solution>.Api/Services/ — business logic behind the controllers
src/<Solution>.Api/Models/ — request/response and data models
tests/<Solution>.Tests/ — xUnit tests mirroring the src layout

testing.kinds: Integration test per endpoint operation (status codes, auth, roles) plus unit tests for services.

rust-cli-app.description: Rust built as a single native executable (CLI/TUI/desktop) with a plain `cargo new` layout — no GUI webview. For a real windowed GUI (a webview-based UI, e.g. Tauri), use tauri-desktop-app instead; that has a materially different required project layout, not just a different UI folder.
fileLayout: src/main.rs — entry point
src/commands/ — one file per CLI command or terminal action
src/ui/ — UI/rendering code, in whichever text-mode toolkit the spec names (e.g. ratatui)
src/db/ — embedded database access, if the solution has one
tests/ — integration tests; unit tests live beside their module with #[cfg(test)]
Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock — project manifest and pinned dependencies

testing.kinds: TDD per the project's dev-rules: a failing test before each new function. Unit tests beside each module; integration tests in tests/ per command or user action from the brief.

tauri-desktop-app.description: A real windowed desktop GUI: a Rust backend (src-tauri/) driving a native window around a web-based frontend (HTML/CSS/JS), via Tauri. Use this — not rust-cli-app — whenever the brief describes an actual window/canvas/drag-and-drop UI rather than a pure terminal tool. An in-app terminal (real shell or otherwise) is a feature rendered inside this same window, not a separate console.

versions.tauri: 2 (pin the exact crate version used in Cargo.toml at scaffold time)
versions.node: 22 LTS (frontend tooling only — not a runtime dependency of the shipped app)

fileLayout: src-tauri/Cargo.toml, src-tauri/tauri.conf.json, src-tauri/build.rs — the Tauri backend manifest and config
src-tauri/src/main.rs — backend entry point; registers Tauri commands the frontend calls
src-tauri/src/commands/ — one file per Tauri command (backend logic invoked from the frontend)
src-tauri/src/db/ — embedded database access, if the solution has one
src-tauri/src/terminal/ — the embedded shell/PTY integration, if the brief calls for a real terminal
src-tauri/tests/ — integration tests; unit tests live beside their module with #[cfg(test)]
src/ — the React + TypeScript frontend (Vite)
src/pages/ — one component per page/screen brief
src/components/ — shared UI components
src/lib/ — shared frontend logic (Tauri command wrappers, theming)
src/pages/__tests__/ — Vitest tests mirroring src/pages/
package.json, vite.config.ts, tsconfig.json — frontend dependencies, build, and TypeScript config

testing.framework: Rust's built-in test framework (cargo test) for src-tauri/; Vitest for the React frontend
testing.kinds: TDD per the project's dev-rules for backend commands: a failing test before each new function. Frontend component tests per screen brief (render, interaction, state) with Vitest.

commands.install: cargo fetch (in src-tauri/) and npm install (frontend)
commands.run: npm run tauri dev — or cargo run from src-tauri/ if only the backend is being exercised
commands.build: npm run tauri build — produces platform-specific release binaries
commands.test: cargo test (from src-tauri/) and npm test (Vitest, frontend)

sql-server-database-project.description: SQL Server database project (DACPAC) — one .sql file per table, deployed with sqlpackage.
fileLayout: Tables/ — one CREATE TABLE .sql per database-model brief
StoredProcedures/ — one .sql per procedure, if any
Scripts/ — post-deployment seeds and reference data
<Solution>.sqlproj — the database project file

testing.framework: tSQLt (or plain verification scripts if tSQLt is unavailable)
testing.kinds: Per model: uniqueness, nullability, defaults, and relationship constraints from the database-model brief.

turso-embedded.description: Turso (libSQL) opened as a local embedded file from the host application — no separate database server or connection string over the network.

versions.turso-crate: latest stable — pin the exact version used in Cargo.toml at scaffold time

fileLayout: src/db/schema.rs (or migrations/) — one migration per database-model brief
src/db/queries.rs — parameterised queries, grouped by model
tests/db/ — one test module per database-model brief: uniqueness, nullability, relationships

testing.framework: Rust's built-in test framework (cargo test), against a temporary database file per test run
testing.kinds: Per model: uniqueness, nullability, defaults, and relationship constraints from the database-model brief.
